content.problemStatement:
You are given a sequence A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​. You need to find the maximum length of a subsequence of AAA whose elements first strictly increase and then strictly decrease in value. Note that subsequences that are only strictly increasing and subsequences that are only strictly decreasing are considered as well.

A subsequence of a given sequence is a sequence that can be derived from the given sequence by deleting some or no elements without changing the order of the remaining elements.


Input Format


The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.




The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN.




The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​.





Output Format

For each test case, print a single line containing one integer ― the maximum length of a subsequence of the sequence AAA which is first strictly increasing and then strictly increasing.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 200000
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer N, the length of the sequence. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1 … AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line with one integer – the maximum possible length of a subsequence that first strictly increases and then strictly decreases (purely increasing or purely decreasing subsequences are also allowed).

sampleTestCases.explanation: For each test case the longest subsequence that first strictly increases then strictly decreases (or only increases/decreases) has lengths 7, 4, 4 and 1 respectively, as described in the statement.

solution.approach: For every position i compute L[i] = length of the longest strictly increasing subsequence that ends at i. This can be done in O(N log N) using the classic patience‑sorting method with a tail array and binary search. Similarly compute R[i] = length of the longest strictly decreasing subsequence that starts at i. R[i] is obtained by running the same LIS algorithm on the reversed array (which gives the longest increasing subsequence in reverse order, i.e. decreasing in the original order) and then mapping the results back. The answer for the test case is max_i (L[i] + R[i] - 1). The subtraction removes the double‑counted element at the peak. The algorithm runs in O(N log N) time and O(N) memory per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case

code.Python3:
import sys

def lis_lengths(arr):
    import bisect
    tail=[]
    res=[0]*len(arr)
    for i,x in enumerate(arr):
        pos=bisect.bisect_left(tail,x)
        if pos==len(tail):
            tail.append(x)
        else:
            tail[pos]=x
        res[i]=pos+1
    return res

def solve():
    data=sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data: return
    it=iter(data)
    T=int(next(it))
    out=[]
    for _ in range(T):
        N=int(next(it))
        a=[int(next(it)) for _ in range(N)]
        L=lis_lengths(a)
        R=lis_lengths(a[::-1])[::-1]
        ans=0
        for l,r in zip(L,R):
            ans=max(ans,l+r-1)
        out.append(str(ans))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out))

if __name__=='__main__':
    solve()
